title: A Decisional 82-Year-Old Woman Insists on Returning Home Alone After a Fall Despite the Team's Recommendation for Assisted Living: Rigorously Assessing Decision-Making Capacity, Honoring Autonomy and the 'Dignity of Risk,' Ensuring an Informed Choice, Maximizing Safety Within Her Decision (Harm Reduction), Avoiding Coercion or Inappropriate APS Referral, and Supporting an Anxious Family
subtitle: A CAPACITATED (decisional) ADULT—at any age—has the legal and ethical right to make decisions about her own life and care, INCLUDING choices that others consider risky or unwise, such as living alone at home despite recognized safety concerns. This case centers on the ETHICAL/LEGAL tension between AUTONOMY (self-determination) and BENEFICENCE/NONMALEFICENCE (the team's wish to protect her), resolved in favor of autonomy when the patient HAS decision-making capacity and is making an INFORMED choice. KEY CONCEPTS: (1) DECISION-MAKING CAPACITY is a CLINICAL determination (distinct from 'competence,' a LEGAL/court determination) that is DECISION-SPECIFIC and PRESUMED in adults; it has FOUR components—(a) UNDERSTANDING the relevant information, (b) APPRECIATING how it applies to one's own situation, (c) REASONING through the options and consequences, and (d) COMMUNICATING a stable choice. CRITICALLY, capacity is NOT negated by old age, mild forgetfulness, an 'eccentric' lifestyle, or simply DISAGREEING with the medical recommendation; making what clinicians regard as an UNWISE choice is not, by itself, evidence of incapacity. Before concluding a patient lacks capacity, RULE OUT and OPTIMIZE reversible contributors—DELIRIUM, DEPRESSION, untreated PAIN, medication/sedation effects, hypoxia/metabolic disturbance, and sensory barriers (uncorrected hearing/vision)—then reassess; capacity can FLUCTUATE, so assess at the patient's best and for the specific decision at hand. (2) The 'DIGNITY OF RISK' principle holds that respecting a person's dignity and personhood includes allowing them to take REASONABLE RISKS and to live according to their own values; over-protective PATERNALISM (overriding a capable person's choice 'for their own good') is not ethically justified and can cause harm (loss of autonomy, dignity, function, and trust). (3) INFORMED DECISION-MAKING: the patient must understand her diagnosis/functional limitations, the recommended plan and its benefits, the RISKS of her preferred plan (e.g., falls, fire, missed medications, functional decline, delayed help), and the ALTERNATIVES—communicated in plain language, with sensory aids (glasses/hearing aids) and a professional interpreter if needed; a truly informed risky choice by a capacitated patient is hers to make. (4) HARM REDUCTION / MAXIMIZE SAFETY WITHIN HER CHOICE: when a capable patient chooses a riskier option (going home), the appropriate response is NOT to coerce or override but to OPTIMIZE safety within that choice through SHARED DECISION-MAKING—PT/OT HOME SAFETY EVALUATION + home modifications (grab bars, remove throw rugs, improve lighting, raised toilet seat, stair rails), a PERSONAL EMERGENCY RESPONSE SYSTEM (medical alert), medication management aids (pill organizers, blister packs, simplified regimen), HOME HEALTH/visiting nurse + therapy, meal and transportation services, caregiver/family support, fall-prevention, and close follow-up—and to offer a time-limited TRIAL at home with reassessment. (5) WHAT NOT TO DO: do NOT COERCE, threaten, manipulate, or shame; do NOT declare the patient 'incompetent' simply because she disagrees; do NOT DETAIN or delay the discharge of a capacitated, informed patient against her will (which can constitute FALSE IMPRISONMENT); and do NOT make an APS (Adult Protective Services) referral for an autonomous, capable adult making an informed risky choice—APS addresses ABUSE, NEGLECT (by others), EXPLOITATION, or SELF-NEGLECT by someone who LACKS capacity, NOT competent self-determination. (6) IF CAPACITY IS GENUINELY LACKING (e.g., advanced dementia, unresolved delirium), the pathway DIFFERS: identify the SURROGATE decision-maker (healthcare proxy/advance directive, or the legal hierarchy), use SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what the patient would have wanted) or, absent that, the BEST-INTEREST standard, consider guardianship if no surrogate, and involve APS for self-neglect when there is no safe plan—so an accurate capacity assessment is pivotal because it determines WHO decides. (7) AGAINST MEDICAL ADVICE (AMA): if a capacitated patient ultimately leaves against advice, the nurse still supports informed decision-making, provides discharge resources/medications/follow-up, documents the discussion objectively, and does NOT abandon or punish the patient ('AMA' does not forfeit care or a safe plan). FAMILY: an anxious family's concerns are valid and should be heard and supported, communication facilitated, and the family engaged in the safety plan—but the DECISION belongs to the capacitated patient, whose wishes about family involvement and privacy are respected. INTERDISCIPLINARY/ETHICS: involve geriatrics, SOCIAL WORK, PT/OT, CASE MANAGEMENT, and an ETHICS CONSULT when there is conflict or uncertainty about capacity; DOCUMENT the capacity assessment, the informed-decision discussion, and the safety plan objectively. NURSING ROLE: assess capacity rigorously (optimizing reversible factors), ADVOCATE for the patient's autonomy and dignity of risk, ensure her choice is informed, build the safest feasible plan within her decision, avoid coercion/inappropriate APS referral, and support the family—balancing safety with self-determination

patient.name: E. Whitman
patient.code: Full code; DECISIONAL adult (capacity intact for this decision); her own decision-maker; widowed; daughter is the listed healthcare proxy but the patient currently has capacity, so SHE decides; goal = respect her autonomy while maximizing her safety at home
patient.admitDate: Admitted 2 days ago after a ground-level FALL at home (mechanical, slipped on a rug; no fracture on imaging, minor hip contusion). She lives ALONE in a two-story house and has had mild functional decline + two prior falls in the past year + mild gait instability. She is medically stable and ready for discharge. The interdisciplinary team and her daughter recommend transition to ASSISTED LIVING (or a short rehab stay), but Ms. Whitman—alert, oriented, and reasoning clearly—firmly INSISTS on returning HOME, stating she has lived there 50 years and would rather accept the risks of home than move. The question is whether to honor her choice, hinging on a careful capacity assessment.
patient.room: Geriatric/medical unit, medically stable for discharge; interdisciplinary team (geriatrics, nursing, PT/OT, social work, case management ± ethics consult) engaged; daughter present and anxious

vitals.bp: 132/78 sitting; 124/74 standing (no significant orthostasis after med review)
vitals.temp: 36.8°C; alert, oriented x4, conversant, reasoning clearly; minor resolving hip contusion (no fracture); independent with transfers + ambulates with a steadying device; no acute medical issue requiring hospitalization; she is calm and consistently states she wants to go home

36.8°C; screened for reversible impairments to capacity — NO delirium (CAM negative), NO untreated pain, NO sedating medications, NO depression on screening, glasses + hearing aid in place; cognitive screen shows mild forgetfulness but she can UNDERSTAND her situation, APPRECIATE the risks, REASON through options, and COMMUNICATE a stable choice = HAS decision-making capacity for this decision

36.8°C; team clearly reviewed her fall risk + functional limitations, the recommendation (assisted living/rehab) + its benefits, the RISKS of returning home (falls, delayed help, fire, missed meds, decline), and ALTERNATIVES; she accurately paraphrases the risks, acknowledges them, and still chooses home ('I understand I might fall again; I would rather live in my own home') = an INFORMED, autonomous choice

vitals.time: Shared safety-planning (harm reduction within her choice)
vitals.temp: 36.8°C; rather than coercing or overriding, the team maximizes safety WITHIN her decision — PT/OT HOME SAFETY evaluation, home modifications (grab bars, remove rugs, lighting, raised toilet, stair rail), a PERSONAL EMERGENCY RESPONSE SYSTEM, medication aids, home health + therapy, meals/transportation services, daughter check-ins, and a time-limited trial with follow-up; she agrees to these supports

36.8°C; anxious daughter's concerns heard + validated; team explains the mother has capacity and the right to decide, and shares the safety plan to reassure the family; NO coercion, NO 'incompetent' label, NO APS referral (she is capable + informed, not abused/neglected/incapacitated); discharged home with services + follow-up + an open door to revisit the plan; capacity assessment + informed-decision discussion + safety plan documented objectively

—; teaching contrast: had the assessment instead revealed she LACKED capacity (e.g., unresolved delirium or advanced dementia making her unable to understand/appreciate/reason), the pathway would differ — identify the SURROGATE (proxy/advance directive or legal hierarchy), use SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT then BEST-INTEREST, consider guardianship if no surrogate, and involve APS for self-neglect if no safe plan; accurate capacity assessment determines WHO decides

labs.v: 82 y/o woman E. Whitman, admitted 2 days ago after a mechanical GROUND-LEVEL FALL at home (slipped on a throw rug; imaging negative for fracture; minor hip contusion). She lives ALONE in a two-story house she has owned for 50 years, with mild functional decline, mild gait instability, and TWO prior falls in the past year. She is now MEDICALLY STABLE and ready for discharge. The interdisciplinary team and her adult daughter are worried about her safety living alone and RECOMMEND a transition to ASSISTED LIVING (or a short rehab stay). However, Ms. Whitman—alert, oriented, conversant, and reasoning clearly—firmly and consistently INSISTS on returning HOME, stating she values her independence and would rather accept the risks of home than move out of her home. PMH: hypertension (controlled), osteoarthritis, mild hearing loss (uses a hearing aid), early/mild memory changes (no formal dementia diagnosis), prior falls. SOCIAL: widowed; one daughter who lives ~30 minutes away and is anxious and protective; a few supportive neighbors; was independent in her ADLs until recently with some new difficulty with stairs/IADLs; previously declined help. The CENTRAL question is ETHICAL/LEGAL: should the team honor her wish to go home, or override it for safety? The answer hinges on whether she has DECISION-MAKING CAPACITY for this decision and is making an INFORMED choice—because a capacitated, informed adult has the right to make choices others consider risky. The nurse's role is to assess capacity rigorously, advocate for her autonomy/dignity, ensure her decision is informed, and maximize safety within whatever she decides—rather than reflexively defaulting to the 'safest' (most restrictive) disposition. This is NOT a case of abuse/neglect or of an incapacitated patient; it is a competent older adult exercising self-determination.
labs.r: A medically stable, decisional 82-year-old wants to go home alone despite fall risk, against the team's/family's recommendation for assisted living. The issue is AUTONOMY vs beneficence, resolved by a rigorous CAPACITY assessment + informed decision-making. RN role: assess capacity, advocate for autonomy/dignity, ensure an informed choice, and maximize safety within her decision—not reflexively choose the most restrictive 'safe' option.

labs.n: Decision-making capacity — the four components + optimizing reversible factors
labs.v: DECISION-MAKING CAPACITY is the linchpin: it determines WHO gets to decide. Capacity is a CLINICAL determination (any licensed clinician can assess it) and is distinct from COMPETENCE (a LEGAL status determined by a court). It is DECISION-SPECIFIC (a person may have capacity for one decision and not another, depending on complexity/consequences) and is PRESUMED present in adults. ASSESS the FOUR COMPONENTS: (1) UNDERSTANDING — can she describe her medical situation, the recommended plan, the risks/benefits, and the alternatives in her own words? (2) APPRECIATION — does she recognize how this information applies to HER specifically (not in the abstract), including that the risks are real for her? (3) REASONING — can she logically weigh the options, manipulate the information, and explain how she arrived at her choice in a way consistent with her values? (4) COMMUNICATION — can she express a CLEAR and reasonably STABLE choice? CRITICAL CAVEATS: capacity is NOT determined by the CONTENT of the decision—an UNWISE or risky choice (going home despite fall risk) by someone who meets the four criteria is still a capacitated choice; old age, mild forgetfulness, eccentricity, a low score on a brief cognitive screen, or simple DISAGREEMENT with the team do NOT by themselves establish incapacity. Before concluding a patient LACKS capacity, IDENTIFY and OPTIMIZE REVERSIBLE contributors: DELIRIUM (screen with CAM and treat causes), DEPRESSION, untreated PAIN, SEDATING medications, hypoxia/metabolic/electrolyte disturbance, infection, and SENSORY barriers (ensure she has her GLASSES and HEARING AIDS and that information is communicated clearly, with an interpreter if needed)—then REASSESS, ideally at her best time of day. Capacity can FLUCTUATE (e.g., 'sundowning,' delirium), so it is assessed for the specific decision, at the relevant time. THIS PATIENT: CAM-negative (no delirium), no untreated pain, no sedating meds, depression screen negative, glasses + hearing aid in use; despite mild forgetfulness she clearly UNDERSTANDS her fall risk and functional limits, APPRECIATES that they apply to her, REASONS through the options consistent with her lifelong value of independence, and COMMUNICATES a stable choice → she HAS capacity for this disposition decision. Therefore, autonomy governs.
labs.r: Capacity (clinical, decision-specific, presumed) = UNDERSTAND + APPRECIATE + REASON + COMMUNICATE a stable choice. An unwise/risky choice, old age, mild forgetfulness, or disagreement do NOT equal incapacity. First optimize reversible factors (delirium, depression, pain, sedating meds, sensory barriers) and reassess. This patient meets all four → she has capacity → she decides. RN role: rigorous, fair capacity assessment.

labs.n: Autonomy, dignity of risk, and informed decision-making
labs.v: Once capacity is established, AUTONOMY (self-determination) governs, guided by the 'DIGNITY OF RISK' principle: respecting a person's dignity includes allowing them to take REASONABLE RISKS and to live by their own values, even when clinicians or family would choose differently. Over-protective PATERNALISM—overriding a capable person's choice 'for their own good'—is ethically unjustified and itself causes harm: loss of autonomy and dignity, accelerated functional decline and institutionalization, depression, and erosion of trust. The competing principle of BENEFICENCE/NONMALEFICENCE (wanting to keep her safe) is real and motivates the team, but it does NOT override the informed choice of a capacitated adult. The nurse's job is to ensure the choice is INFORMED—not to change it: clearly review (a) her diagnosis and functional limitations, (b) the recommended plan (assisted living/rehab) and its benefits, (c) the RISKS of her preferred plan of going home alone (recurrent falls, prolonged 'down time' before help arrives, fire/cooking hazards, missed or mismanaged medications, progressive decline, social isolation), and (d) the ALTERNATIVES and supports available—using plain language, ensuring her glasses/hearing aids are in place, allowing questions, and using a professional interpreter if there is any language barrier (never family for medical-risk discussions). The nurse confirms understanding (teach-back) and explores her values and goals (what matters most to her). THIS PATIENT accurately paraphrases the risks, explicitly acknowledges she might fall again, and still chooses home because remaining in her own home is, to her, worth the risk—an INFORMED, autonomous decision. Respecting it is not 'letting her fail'; it is honoring her personhood. The nurse documents the informed-decision discussion and supports her choice while building the safest feasible plan around it.
labs.r: With capacity established, autonomy + the DIGNITY OF RISK govern: a capable person may take reasonable risks per their own values; paternalism is unjustified and harmful. The nurse ensures the choice is INFORMED (diagnosis, recommendation + benefits, risks of her plan, alternatives — plain language + sensory aids + teach-back + explore values), not changed. This patient makes an informed choice to go home. RN role: ensure informed decision-making + respect autonomy.

labs.n: Harm reduction — maximizing safety within her decision
labs.v: Respecting autonomy does NOT mean discharging her with no plan; the ethical AND practical response to a capable patient's riskier choice is HARM REDUCTION—maximizing safety WITHIN her decision through SHARED DECISION-MAKING. Mobilize the interdisciplinary team to build a robust home plan: (1) PT/OT HOME SAFETY EVALUATION (ideally a home visit) to assess hazards and function and recommend modifications; (2) HOME MODIFICATIONS to reduce fall risk—install GRAB BARS (bathroom), REMOVE THROW RUGS and clutter, improve LIGHTING (including night lights), add STAIR RAILS/consider relocating essential activities to one level, a RAISED TOILET SEAT/shower chair, and nonslip footwear; (3) a PERSONAL EMERGENCY RESPONSE SYSTEM (medical-alert button/wearable) so she can summon help quickly if she falls (addresses the 'long lie' risk); (4) MEDICATION management supports—pill organizer/blister packs, simplified regimen, pharmacy/medication review (deprescribe fall-risk-increasing drugs where possible), and reminders; (5) HOME HEALTH services—visiting nurse + home PT/OT, and a home-health aide for ADL/IADL support as accepted; (6) community services—MEALS (e.g., Meals on Wheels), TRANSPORTATION, and social engagement to counter isolation; (7) FAMILY/neighbor support—scheduled check-ins (in-person/phone), a contact tree, and (with her permission) educating the daughter on warning signs; (8) FALL-PREVENTION counseling + vision/hearing optimization + appropriate assistive device; (9) a time-limited TRIAL at home with close FOLLOW-UP (e.g., a clinic visit and home-health assessment within days) and an explicit, non-coercive OPEN DOOR to revisit the plan if her situation changes or she chooses differently. This approach honors her autonomy while genuinely reducing risk—and often a well-supported home plan is safer than a forced move she will resist. The nurse coordinates case management/social work to arrange and verify these services before discharge. THIS PATIENT agreed to PT/OT home eval, home modifications, a personal emergency response system, medication aids, home health, meals/transportation, daughter check-ins, and early follow-up.
labs.r: Harm reduction = maximize safety WITHIN her choice via shared decision-making: PT/OT home safety eval + home modifications (grab bars, remove rugs, lighting, stair rails, raised toilet), a PERSONAL EMERGENCY RESPONSE SYSTEM, medication aids + review, home health + aide, meals/transportation, family check-ins, fall prevention, and a time-limited trial with close follow-up + an open door. RN role: coordinate a robust, autonomy-respecting safety plan before discharge.

labs.n: What NOT to do + when capacity is lacking + family; Pertinent NEGATIVES
labs.v: WHAT NOT TO DO with a capacitated, informed patient: do NOT COERCE, threaten, manipulate, guilt, or shame her into accepting placement; do NOT label her 'incompetent'/'noncompliant' merely because she disagrees with the recommendation; do NOT DETAIN her or delay her discharge against her will once she is capacitated and informed (unlawful detention of a capable patient can constitute FALSE IMPRISONMENT); do NOT make an ADULT PROTECTIVE SERVICES (APS) referral for an autonomous, capable adult making an informed risky choice—APS exists to investigate ABUSE, NEGLECT (by a caregiver), EXPLOITATION, or SELF-NEGLECT by a person who LACKS capacity; a capable person choosing to accept risk in her own home is NOT a basis for APS; and do NOT exclude her from the decision or negotiate only with the family. WHEN CAPACITY IS GENUINELY LACKING (the contrasting pathway—e.g., advanced dementia or unresolved delirium where she cannot understand/appreciate/reason): the decision shifts to a SURROGATE—first an appointed HEALTHCARE PROXY/advance directive, then the legal SURROGATE HIERARCHY (often spouse → adult children → etc., per state law)—who applies SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what the patient would have chosen based on her known values) and, if those are unknown, the BEST-INTEREST standard; if there is no surrogate, GUARDIANSHIP may be pursued; and APS/self-neglect involvement is appropriate when an incapacitated person has no safe plan. Thus the capacity assessment is decisive because it determines WHO decides. FAMILY: the daughter's worry is legitimate and should be heard, validated, and supported; facilitate communication and (with the patient's permission) engage the family in the safety plan—but the DECISION belongs to the capacitated patient, and her wishes about family involvement/privacy are respected. ETHICS/INTERDISCIPLINARY: involve geriatrics, social work, PT/OT, case management, and an ETHICS CONSULT for conflict or genuine uncertainty about capacity; DOCUMENT objectively—the capacity assessment, the informed-decision discussion (risks reviewed + her understanding + her decision in her own words), the safety plan, and follow-up. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): she was NOT coerced or detained; NOT labeled incompetent for disagreeing; NO inappropriate APS referral (no abuse/neglect/incapacity); reversible capacity barriers were excluded/optimized BEFORE judging capacity; she was NOT excluded from her own decision; and she was NOT discharged without a safety plan. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: intact, carefully assessed capacity; an informed, values-based choice; a robust harm-reduction home plan; an engaged (if anxious) daughter + neighbors; interdisciplinary + ethics support; objective documentation; and a non-coercive open door to revisit. EXPECTED outcome: she returns home with maximized supports and close follow-up, her autonomy and dignity preserved, with the plan re-evaluated as her needs change—demonstrating that the goal is to balance SAFETY with SELF-DETERMINATION, not to default to the most restrictive option.
labs.r: Do NOT coerce/detain (false imprisonment), label 'incompetent' for disagreeing, or refer APS for a capable adult's informed risky choice (APS = abuse/neglect/exploitation/self-neglect-with-incapacity). If capacity is LACKING → surrogate + substituted judgment → best interest → guardianship/APS. Support (don't defer to) the anxious family; ethics consult for conflict; document objectively. Negatives confirm correct practice. RN role: protect autonomy + safety + family support, balancing self-determination with safety.

nursesNote: Geriatric unit, hospital day 2 — 82 y/o widow E. Whitman, admitted after a mechanical ground-level FALL at home (slipped on a rug; no fracture; minor hip contusion). Lives ALONE in a 2-story home of 50 years; mild functional decline + mild gait instability + 2 prior falls this year + mild memory changes (no dementia dx) + mild hearing loss (hearing aid). Now MEDICALLY STABLE for discharge. Team + anxious daughter recommend ASSISTED LIVING/short rehab; Ms. Whitman—alert, oriented x4, reasoning clearly—firmly + consistently INSISTS on going HOME, valuing her independence. CENTRAL issue = AUTONOMY vs beneficence, decided by CAPACITY + informed decision-making. CAPACITY ASSESSMENT (optimize reversible factors FIRST): CAM-negative (no delirium), no untreated pain, no sedating meds, depression screen negative, glasses + hearing aid in place, communicated clearly; despite mild forgetfulness she can UNDERSTAND her fall risk/functional limits, APPRECIATE that they apply to HER, REASON through options consistent with her lifelong value of independence, and COMMUNICATE a STABLE choice → HAS decision-making capacity for this decision (capacity is clinical, decision-specific, presumed; an unwise/risky choice + old age + mild forgetfulness + disagreement do NOT equal incapacity). INFORMED-DECISION discussion: team reviewed diagnosis/functional limits, the recommendation (assisted living/rehab) + benefits, the RISKS of home (recurrent falls, long down-time before help, fire/cooking hazards, missed meds, decline, isolation), and ALTERNATIVES, in plain language with sensory aids + teach-back; she accurately paraphrases the risks, explicitly acknowledges she might fall again, and still chooses home ('I'd rather live in my own home') = INFORMED, autonomous choice; DIGNITY OF RISK respected (paternalism avoided). HARM REDUCTION (maximize safety WITHIN her choice, shared decision-making): PT/OT HOME SAFETY evaluation; home modifications (grab bars, remove throw rugs, lighting/night lights, stair rail, raised toilet seat, nonslip footwear); PERSONAL EMERGENCY RESPONSE SYSTEM (medical alert) for fall/long-lie risk; medication aids + review (deprescribe fall-risk meds where possible); HOME HEALTH (visiting nurse + home PT/OT + aide as accepted); meals/transportation services + social engagement; daughter/neighbor check-ins (with her permission); fall-prevention + vision/hearing optimization + assistive device; time-limited TRIAL with early follow-up + an OPEN DOOR to revisit — she agreed to these supports. FAMILY MEETING: daughter's worry heard + validated; explained the mother has capacity + the right to decide; shared the safety plan to reassure; NO coercion, NO 'incompetent' label, NO detention/discharge delay against her will (avoid false imprisonment), NO APS referral (she is capable + informed, not abused/neglected/incapacitated). DISCHARGED home with services + follow-up; capacity assessment + informed-decision discussion + safety plan documented objectively. CONTRAST (teaching): had she LACKED capacity (unresolved delirium/advanced dementia) → surrogate (proxy/advance directive or legal hierarchy) + SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT → BEST INTEREST → guardianship if no surrogate, + APS for self-neglect if no safe plan. Ethics consult available for conflict/uncertainty. Outcome: autonomy + dignity preserved with maximized safety + close follow-up + re-evaluation as needs change.
providerOrders: CONTINUE: geriatric/medical unit; medically stable post-fall (no fracture); plan discharge disposition
DIAGNOSIS: mechanical fall in an 82-year-old living alone with mild functional decline; disposition decision with autonomy-vs-safety considerations; decision-making capacity assessment
CODE STATUS: full code; decisional adult (capacity intact) — patient is her own decision-maker; daughter is listed proxy (applies only if she loses capacity)
CAPACITY ASSESSMENT:
Assess the 4 components (understand, appreciate, reason, communicate a stable choice) for the specific disposition decision; document

Optimize/exclude reversible contributors first: screen for delirium (CAM), depression, untreated pain, sedating medications, hypoxia/metabolic disturbance, infection; ensure glasses + hearing aids; use interpreter if needed; reassess at her best
Do NOT equate disagreement, old age, mild forgetfulness, or an 'unwise' choice with incapacity
IF CAPACITY INTACT (this patient) — honor autonomy:

Ensure an INFORMED decision: review diagnosis/functional limits, the recommended plan + benefits, the risks of going home (falls, delayed help, fire, missed meds, decline, isolation), and alternatives, in plain language + teach-back; explore her values/goals
Respect the dignity of risk; do NOT coerce, threaten, shame, label 'incompetent,' detain, or delay discharge against her will; do NOT refer APS for a capable, informed risky choice
Support an AMA departure if she chooses it (provide resources, prescriptions, follow-up; document; do not abandon)
HARM REDUCTION — maximize safety WITHIN her choice (shared decision-making):
PT/OT home safety evaluation (home visit if possible) + assistive device
Home modifications: grab bars, remove throw rugs/clutter, improve lighting/night lights, stair rails, raised toilet seat/shower chair, nonslip footwear
Personal emergency response system (medical-alert) for fall/long-lie risk
Medication management: pill organizer/blister packs, simplify regimen, pharmacy review + deprescribe fall-risk-increasing medications where possible
Home health (visiting nurse + home PT/OT + aide as accepted); meals + transportation services; social engagement
Family/neighbor check-ins (with patient permission); educate daughter on warning signs
Time-limited trial at home with early follow-up (clinic + home-health within days) and an open, non-coercive door to revisit the plan
IF CAPACITY LACKING (contrast pathway):

Identify surrogate (healthcare proxy/advance directive, then legal hierarchy); use substituted judgment then best-interest standard; consider guardianship if no surrogate; involve APS for self-neglect if no safe plan
INTERDISCIPLINARY + FAMILY + DOCUMENTATION:
Geriatrics + social work + PT/OT + case management; ethics consult for conflict or uncertainty about capacity
Hear + validate + support the anxious family; engage them (with permission) in the safety plan; the decision remains the capacitated patient's
Document objectively: capacity assessment, informed-decision discussion (risks reviewed + her understanding + her decision in her words), safety plan, and follow-up

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in this disposition decision?
steps.inst: Select ALL considerations that are clinically and ethically significant.

opts.text: A medically stable, decisional adult is making a choice (go home alone) that the team/family consider risky — the core issue is autonomy vs beneficence, decided by capacity + informed decision-making
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — framing the ethical question correctly drives the whole approach. RN role: recognize autonomy-vs-safety + assess capacity.

opts.text: Decision-making capacity must be assessed for this specific decision (understand, appreciate, reason, communicate a stable choice) — and is presumed in adults
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — capacity determines who decides. RN role: rigorous, decision-specific capacity assessment.

opts.text: Reversible contributors to apparent incapacity (delirium, depression, pain, sedating meds, sensory barriers) must be optimized/excluded before judging capacity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — don't mislabel a reversible/correctable state as incapacity. RN role: optimize reversible factors + reassess.

opts.text: An unwise or risky choice, old age, mild forgetfulness, or disagreeing with the team do NOT, by themselves, indicate incapacity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — capacity is about the process, not the content of the choice. RN role: avoid conflating disagreement with incapacity.

opts.text: If she has capacity, the dignity of risk applies — she may take reasonable risks per her values, and the team ensures the choice is informed rather than overriding it
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — autonomy + dignity of risk govern a capable patient's choice. RN role: ensure informed decision-making + respect autonomy.

opts.text: Maximize safety within her decision (harm reduction): home safety eval, modifications, medical-alert system, medication aids, home health, family check-ins, follow-up
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — respecting autonomy still requires a robust safety plan. RN role: coordinate harm-reduction supports.

opts.text: Hear and support the anxious daughter, but recognize the decision belongs to the capacitated patient
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — family support matters, but the capable patient decides. RN role: support family + protect the patient's authority.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Because going home alone is unsafe, the team should place her in assisted living against her wishes for her own good
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — overriding a capacitated, informed adult's choice is unjustified paternalism (and detaining/forcing placement can be unlawful). If she has capacity, honor her decision and maximize safety within it.

opts.text: Refer her to Adult Protective Services because she is an elder choosing a risky living situation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — APS addresses abuse, neglect, exploitation, or self-neglect by someone who LACKS capacity. A capable adult making an informed risky choice is not an APS matter.

opts.text: Capacity is a clinical, decision-specific determination (presumed in adults) assessed by understanding + appreciation + reasoning + communicating a stable choice; competence is a separate legal/court determination
opts.rat: CORRECT — capacity framework + capacity-vs-competence distinction. Foundation for who decides.

opts.text: A capacitated adult may make choices others consider unwise/risky (dignity of risk); paternalistic override is not justified and can cause harm
opts.rat: CORRECT — autonomy + dignity of risk principle. Foundation for honoring her choice.

opts.text: Before concluding incapacity, optimize reversible factors (delirium, depression, pain, sedation, sensory barriers); capacity can fluctuate and is assessed at the patient's best for the specific decision
opts.rat: CORRECT — reversible-factor + fluctuation principle. Foundation for a fair assessment.

opts.text: Respecting autonomy still requires harm reduction — maximizing safety within her choice through home modifications, a medical-alert system, services, and follow-up
opts.rat: CORRECT — harm-reduction principle. Foundation for a safe, autonomy-respecting plan.

opts.text: APS is for abuse, neglect, exploitation, or self-neglect by an incapacitated person — not for a capable adult's informed risky choice; and detaining a capacitated patient against her will can be false imprisonment
opts.rat: CORRECT — APS scope + false-imprisonment principle. Foundation for what NOT to do.

opts.text: If capacity is genuinely lacking, decisions shift to a surrogate using substituted judgment, then best-interest, with guardianship/APS as needed
opts.rat: CORRECT — the lacking-capacity pathway. Foundation for the contrasting approach.

opts.text: Scoring low on a brief cognitive screen (e.g., mild forgetfulness) automatically means a patient lacks capacity to make any decisions
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a cognitive screen is not a capacity determination; capacity is decision-specific and based on the four components. Mild forgetfulness does not equal global incapacity.

opts.text: When a competent patient's choice conflicts with the family's wishes, the family's preference should prevail because they know best
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a capacitated patient's own informed decision governs; the family is supported and engaged (with permission) but does not override the patient.

opts.text: Assess decision-making capacity for this decision, first optimizing reversible contributors (delirium, depression, pain, sedation, sensory barriers)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Capacity determines who decides; it must be assessed (and reversible factors optimized) before anything else follows. RN role: rigorous capacity assessment.

opts.text: Ensure an informed decision — review the diagnosis, recommendation + benefits, risks of her plan, and alternatives in plain language with teach-back; explore her values
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. A valid autonomous choice must be informed. RN role: informed decision-making.

opts.text: Respect autonomy / dignity of risk — honor her informed choice without coercion, detention, an 'incompetent' label, or an inappropriate APS referral
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Once capacity + informed choice are established, autonomy governs. RN role: protect autonomy; avoid coercion/false imprisonment/APS misuse.

opts.text: Maximize safety within her decision — coordinate home safety eval, modifications, a medical-alert system, medication aids, home health, services, and follow-up
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Harm reduction makes her chosen plan as safe as feasible. RN role: build the safety plan.

opts.text: Support the anxious family and use ethics/interdisciplinary resources; document the assessment, discussion, and plan
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout. Family support + ethics + documentation round out the response. RN role: family support + coordination + documentation.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED.

opts.text: Assess capacity (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate) after optimizing reversible factors (CAM for delirium, depression/pain screen, review sedating meds, ensure glasses/hearing aids)
opts.rat: Correct, fair capacity assessment. RN role: assess capacity + optimize reversible factors.

opts.text: Provide a clear informed-decision discussion (diagnosis, recommendation + benefits, risks of home, alternatives) with teach-back and explore her values
opts.rat: Ensures the autonomous choice is informed. RN role: informed decision-making.

opts.text: Honor her informed choice to return home and coordinate a robust safety plan (PT/OT home eval, modifications, medical-alert, medication aids, home health, services, follow-up)
opts.rat: Respects autonomy + maximizes safety within her decision. RN role: support choice + harm reduction.

opts.text: Hear and validate the daughter's concerns and engage her (with the patient's permission) in the safety plan
opts.rat: Supports the family while preserving the patient's authority. RN role: family support.

opts.text: Offer a time-limited trial at home with early follow-up and a non-coercive open door to revisit the plan
opts.rat: Balances autonomy with ongoing safety reassessment. RN role: trial + follow-up + open door.

opts.text: Request an ethics consult and document the capacity assessment, informed-decision discussion, and safety plan objectively
opts.rat: Appropriate support + documentation. RN role: ethics/interdisciplinary + objective documentation.

opts.text: Override her wishes and arrange involuntary placement in assisted living for her own safety
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — overriding a capacitated, informed adult is unjustified paternalism; forcing placement/detaining her can be unlawful (false imprisonment).

opts.text: Document her as 'incompetent and noncompliant' because she refuses the recommended placement
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — disagreement is not incapacity, and the label is inaccurate + judgmental. Capacity must be properly assessed; honor a capable choice.

opts.text: Refer her to Adult Protective Services for choosing to live alone with fall risk
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — APS is for abuse/neglect/exploitation/self-neglect with incapacity, not a capable adult's informed risky choice.

opts.text: Delay her discharge indefinitely and keep her in the hospital until she agrees to placement
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — detaining a capacitated, informed patient against her will can constitute false imprisonment; she may not be held to coerce agreement.

opts.text: Negotiate the discharge plan only with the daughter and proceed with whatever the daughter decides
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the capacitated patient is the decision-maker; excluding her and deferring to family violates her autonomy.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Optimize reversible factors and assess decision-making capacity for the disposition decision
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Capacity determines who decides; assess it (after optimizing reversible factors) before proceeding. RN role: capacity assessment.

opts.text: Provide an informed-decision discussion (risks/benefits/alternatives) with teach-back and explore her values/goals
opts.rat: #2 — Second. A valid autonomous choice must be informed. RN role: informed decision-making.

opts.text: Honor her informed choice and coordinate the interdisciplinary harm-reduction safety plan (home eval, modifications, medical-alert, medication aids, home health, services)
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Respect autonomy + make her plan as safe as feasible. RN role: support choice + build safety plan.

opts.text: Hold a family meeting to hear/validate the daughter and share the safety plan; use an ethics consult if there is conflict/uncertainty
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Support the family + resolve conflict without overriding the patient. RN role: family support + ethics.

opts.text: Discharge home with services + a time-limited trial + early follow-up + an open door, and document objectively
opts.rat: #5 — Last. Execute the plan with reassessment built in. RN role: discharge coordination + follow-up + documentation.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action taken, classify as Appropriate or Inappropriate.

opts.text: The nurse optimized reversible factors (CAM-negative, no untreated pain/sedation, depression screen negative, glasses/hearing aid) and assessed the four capacity components for the specific decision
opts.rat: Correct, fair, decision-specific capacity assessment. Model handling.

opts.text: After confirming capacity, the team ensured an informed decision (reviewed risks/benefits/alternatives with teach-back) and honored her choice to go home
opts.rat: Informed decision-making + respect for autonomy/dignity of risk. Model handling.

opts.text: A robust harm-reduction plan was arranged (home safety eval, grab bars/rug removal/lighting, medical-alert system, medication aids, home health, meals, follow-up)
opts.rat: Maximizing safety within her choice. Model handling.

opts.text: The daughter's concerns were heard and validated, and she was engaged (with permission) in the safety plan, while the decision remained the patient's
opts.rat: Family support without overriding the capacitated patient. Model handling.

opts.text: Because she refused placement, the team documented her as 'incompetent' and arranged involuntary transfer to assisted living
opts.rat: ⚠️ Disagreement is not incapacity; labeling her incompetent and forcing placement overrides a capable adult and can be unlawful. Incorrect.

opts.text: The team kept her hospitalized against her will until she agreed to placement
opts.rat: ⚠️ Detaining a capacitated, informed patient to coerce agreement can constitute false imprisonment. Incorrect.

opts.text: An APS referral was filed because she is an elder choosing to live alone with fall risk
opts.rat: ⚠️ APS is for abuse/neglect/exploitation/self-neglect with incapacity — not a capable adult's informed risky choice. Incorrect.

opts.text: An ethics consult was used given the family conflict, and the capacity assessment, informed-decision discussion, and safety plan were documented objectively
opts.rat: Appropriate use of ethics resources + objective documentation. Model handling.

opts.text: She was discharged home with services, a time-limited trial, early follow-up, and an open door to revisit the plan if her needs change
opts.rat: Autonomy-respecting plan with built-in reassessment. Model handling.

opts.text: In a contrasting patient who truly lacked capacity (unresolved delirium), the team identified the surrogate and used substituted judgment / best-interest rather than the patient's stated wish
opts.rat: Correct pathway when capacity is genuinely lacking — surrogate + substituted judgment/best-interest. Demonstrates that the capacity assessment determines who decides.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
